links.targetTitle: The Swarm plugin mirrors the Switchboard duplication pattern, swapping the remote layer
Connect-on-Swarm data spans four distinct storage layers with different durability
Connect-on-Swarm data spans four distinct storage layers with different durability
Live collaboration uses per-user op feeds plus GSOC pings to trigger debounced pulls.

global.title: Collaboration adds SyncEnvelope wrappers and per-user feeds but does not re-duplicate raw ops
global.content:
Collaboration adds per-user op feeds on top of the single-user sync — and the additional storage cost is small and structural rather than full re-duplication:

**Already present (single-user baseline)**
- PGlite: all operations (local + remote)
- Swarm /bytes: operation batches (per user)
- Single-user feeds: doc/drive/user manifests (personal backup)

**New in collaboration**
- Collab index feed: 1 per shared document (~1 KB)
- Per-user op feeds: 1 per user per document
  - Content: `SyncEnvelope` wrapping the SAME operations that are already in the single-user /bytes batches

**What's actually added**:
- Feed pointer updates: ~64 bytes per update per user
- SyncEnvelope wrappers around operations (OperationContext metadata)

**Overhead quantified**: ~20% per op for the context metadata. The core action data itself is not duplicated — only the wrapping envelope.

**Why it matters**: this means collaboration scales linearly in users rather than in users x ops. Adding N collaborators to a document doesn't N-duplicate the ops; it adds N small-wrapped pointers over the same underlying content-addressed operation chunks.
global.description: Per-user op feeds wrap the same operations in SyncEnvelope context metadata (~20% per-op overhead) plus small feed pointers — the core action data isn't duplicated.
